subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 5, Year 4 mathematicians! In this unit you will grow into a confident, quick and clever multiplier. You already know that multiplication is a fast way of adding equal groups. Now you will polish your times tables, master the tricky 7 times table, and discover two powerful new ideas that mathematicians use every day: multiples and factors. These two words are opposites of each other, and once you understand them you will be able to solve puzzles that look hard but are really quite simple.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Recall and use multiplication facts up to 12 × 12, with special confidence in the 7 times table.
Identify and generate multiples of any given number.
Find and list all the factors of a given number in a systematic way.
Find common multiples of two or more numbers, and name the lowest common multiple (LCM).
Find common factors of two or more numbers, and name the highest common factor (HCF).
Use arrays to picture multiplication, factors and factor pairs.
Apply efficient mental multiplication strategies such as partitioning, doubling and halving, and the distributive property.
Solve multi-step and real-world problems involving multiplication, factors and multiples.

concepts.title: Consolidating the Times Tables
concepts.explanation: Let us begin by strengthening what you already know. Multiplication is a quick way of adding the same number several times. Instead of writing 4 + 4 + 4, we simply write 3 × 4, which means 3 groups of 4. Reading it as groups keeps the meaning clear: 3 × 4 = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12, and 5 × 6 = 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 + 6 = 30. Every multiplication fact is really a shortcut for repeated addition. You have already learned many tables. Here they are laid out so you can check your fluency. Try to read each row aloud without pausing –…

concepts.title: Mastering the 7 Times Table
concepts.explanation: The 7 times table is the one most pupils find the hardest, so let us give it special attention. Once you conquer the sevens, you will feel that no table can defeat you. Here is the whole table, written out in full: Here are some friendly memory tricks that pupils at Nuur Academy use to lock the hardest sevens into their minds:
concepts.example: 7 × 5 = 35 – think, I am 7, I am alive, I am 35!

concepts.title: Understanding Multiples
concepts.explanation: A multiple of a number is what you get when you multiply that number by a whole number (1, 2, 3, 4 and so on). Multiples are simply the answers in a times table. For example, the multiples of 4 are found like this: 4 × 1 = 4, 4 × 2 = 8, 4 × 3 = 12, 4 × 4 = 16, 4 × 5 = 20, and so on. So the multiples of 4 are 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, 40, … They go on forever.
concepts.example: In the same way, the multiples of 7 are 7 × 1 = 7, 7 × 2 = 14, 7 × 3 = 21, 7 × 4 = 28, 7 × 5 = 35, and so on, giving 7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42, 49, 56, 63, 70, … Do you notice that this is exactly the 7 times table again?…

concepts.title: Understanding Factors
concepts.explanation: Factors are the opposite of multiples. A factor is a number that divides exactly into another number, leaving no remainder. If you can share a number into equal groups with nothing left over, the size of the group and the number of groups are both factors. Let us find all the factors of 12. We ask: which numbers divide 12 exactly?

concepts.title: Using Arrays to Picture Factors
concepts.explanation: An array is a rectangular arrangement of objects in equal rows and columns. Arrays are wonderful for *seeing* multiplication and factors, because the number of rows and the number of columns are always a factor pair of the total. Let us picture the factors of 12 with arrays of stars. Each different rectangle shows a different factor pair:
concepts.example: Each rectangle uses all 12 stars but arranges them differently. The rows and columns give the factor pairs: 1 and 12, 2 and 6, 3 and 4. Reading them all off, the factors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 and 12 – exactly what we…

concepts.title: The Link Between Factors, Multiples and Division
concepts.explanation: Factors and multiples are two sides of the same coin, and they are tied tightly to division. Hold on to these two golden sentences: If a × b = c, then a and b are factors of c, and c is a multiple of both a and b.
concepts.example: If a is a factor of b, then b is a multiple of a.

methods.title: How to find all the factors of a number
steps: Start with 1, and pair it with the number itself.
Try 2, then 3, then 4 and so on, in order.
Each time a number divides exactly, write it and its partner as a pair.
Stop when the pairs begin to repeat. List every factor from smallest to largest.

methods.title: How to find the LCM of two numbers
methods.example: Write the first six multiples of 8.
steps: List the multiples of the larger number.
Check each one to see if the smaller number divides it exactly.
The first multiple that works is the lowest common multiple.

methods.title: How to find the HCF of two numbers
steps: List all the factors of each number.
Circle the factors that appear in both lists (the common factors).
The largest circled number is the highest common factor.

methods.title: How to multiply cleverly
steps: Partition: split a number into friendly parts (28 = 20 + 8).
Round and adjust: 7 × 9 = 7 × 10 − 7.
Double and halve: 4 × 15 = 2 × 30.
Use known facts: 8 × 7 = 5 × 7 + 3 × 7.

methods.title: A common multiple problem
methods.example: Two bells ring together at 9:00 AM. One rings every 8 minutes, the other every 12 minutes. When do they next ring together?
steps: Solution: Find the LCM of 8 and 12.
Multiples of 8: 8, 16, 24, 32, … Multiples of 12: 12, 24, 36, … The lowest common multiple is 24.
So the bells ring together every 24 minutes, and next ring together at 9:24 AM.

methods.title: A common factor problem
methods.example: A baker has 24 chocolate cookies and 36 vanilla cookies. She wants to fill boxes so that each box holds the same number of cookies and only one type. What is the largest number she can put in each box?
steps: Solution: We need the largest number that divides both 24 and 36 – the HCF.
The highest common factor is 12.

workedExamples.title: A 7 times table fact
workedExamples.solution: Solution: If you do not recall it instantly, use known facts. 5 × 7 = 35 and 3 × 7 = 21, and 5 + 3 = 8, so 8 × 7 = 35 + 21 = 56. You can check with commutativity: 7 × 8 is the same, and 7 × 8 = 56. Both agree.

workedExamples.title: Listing multiples
workedExamples.prompt: Write the first six multiples of 8.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Multiply 8 by 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6: 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48. So the first six multiples of 8 are 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48. Notice they are all even, because 8 itself is even.

workedExamples.title: Finding all factors
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Work in pairs from 1 upwards. 1 × 30, 2 × 15, 3 × 10, 5 × 6. (4 does not divide 30 exactly, so it is skipped.) After 5 × 6 the pairs would repeat, so we stop. The factors of 30 are 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 15 and 30.

workedExamples.title: Using a strategy for a bigger product
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Partition 28 into 20 + 8. Then 7 × 20 = 140 and 7 × 8 = 56. Add them: 140 + 56 = 196. This is the distributive property doing the hard work for you.

workedExamples.title: A common multiple problem
workedExamples.prompt: Two bells ring together at 9:00 AM. One rings every 8 minutes, the other every 12 minutes. When do they next ring together?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the LCM of 8 and 12. Multiples of 8: 8, 16, 24, 32, … Multiples of 12: 12, 24, 36, … The lowest common multiple is 24. So the bells ring together every 24 minutes, and next ring together at 9:24 AM.

workedExamples.title: A common factor problem
workedExamples.prompt: A baker has 24 chocolate cookies and 36 vanilla cookies. She wants to fill boxes so that each box holds the same number of cookies and only one type. What is the largest number she can put in each box?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: We need the largest number that divides both 24 and 36 – the HCF. Factors of 24: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24. Factors of 36: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 36. The highest common factor is 12. So she puts 12 cookies in each box, giving 2 boxes of chocolate and 3 boxes of vanilla.

workedExamples.title: Factors solving division
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ask, what times 7 makes 63? From the sevens table, 9 × 7 = 63. So 7 is a factor of 63 and 63 ÷ 7 = 9. Division and multiplication are inverse operations.

workedExamples.title: A multi-step word problem
workedExamples.prompt: A school has 8 classes with 26 pupils in each class. How many pupils are there in total?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Total = 8 × 26. Partition 26 into 20 + 6: 8 × 20 = 160 and 8 × 6 = 48. Add: 160 + 48 = 208 pupils. Always show the strategy so a marker can follow your thinking.

workedExamples.title: Use a strategy to work out
workedExamples.prompt: Use a strategy to work out: a) 7 × 9 (round and adjust) b) 4 × 15 (double and halve) c) 8 × 7 (known facts).

workedExamples.title: Use factors to solve
workedExamples.prompt: Use factors to solve: a) 63 ÷ 7 b) 48 ÷ 6 c) 72 ÷ 8.

workedExamples.title: Find the common factors of 20 and 30
workedExamples.prompt: Find the common factors of 20 and 30.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Find the HCF of 24 and 36. Show the factor lists you used.

activities.title: Build an Array Garden
activities.materials: 24 small stones, seeds or bottle caps.
steps: Take your 24 objects and arrange them into a neat rectangle with equal rows.
Write down the array you made, for example 4 rows of 6, and the factor pair it shows (4 and 6).
Now rearrange the same 24 objects into a different rectangle and record that factor pair too.
Keep going until you have found every rectangle. How many factor pairs of 24 did you find?
This shows you why arrays and factors belong together: every tidy rectangle is a factor pair.

activities.title: The 7 Times Table Race
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a family member to time you.
steps: Write the numbers 1 to 12 in a column.
Ask someone to time you as you write the answer to each number times 7.
Check your answers against the table in your Lesson book and note any you missed.
Rest, then try again and see if you can beat your own time. Practise the tricky ones each day.

activities.title: Factor and Multiple Sorting Game
activities.materials: paper cards with numbers written on them (for example 12, 18, 24, 30, 36).
steps: Choose a target number such as 6.
Sort your cards into two piles: numbers that are multiples of 6, and numbers that are not.
Now pick a number like 24 and list all its factor cards from your set.
Swap with a partner and check each other's sorting. Talk about any you disagree on.

activities.title: The Ringing Bells Investigation
activities.materials: paper and pencil, and two different tapping sounds (or two people).
steps: Let one person tap every 4 beats and another tap every 6 beats, counting the beats out loud together.
Mark the beat numbers where both taps land at the same time.
Write down these common beats – they are the common multiples of 4 and 6.
What is the very first beat where they tap together? That number is the LCM. Try it again with 3 and 5.

activities.title: Market Baskets (Family Task)
activities.materials: two piles of small objects, for example 24 dates and 36 groundnuts.
steps: Imagine you are at the Hargeisa market packing gift baskets for Eid.
Each basket must hold the same number of dates and the same number of groundnuts, using everything up.
With your family, work out the largest number of baskets you can make (the HCF of 24 and 36).
Share out the objects into that many baskets and check none are left over.

activities.title: Strategy Showcase
steps: Pick a multiplication such as 7 × 9 or 8 × 6.
Show it solved in two different ways – for example, partitioning and rounding-and-adjusting.
Explain in your own words which method you found quicker and why.
Teach your favourite strategy to a family member and let them try one.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand factors best? What was the trickiest part? Do you now feel quicker at the 7 times table? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

terms: A number that divides another exactly
The result of a times-table step
Two factors that multiply to the number
Objects in equal rows and columns
A factor shared by two numbers
A multiple shared by two numbers
commonMistakes: Factors are few and small; multiples grow forever
HCF for biggest equal groups; LCM for meeting again
Doubling and halving the same number
Double one number, halve the other

realProblems.prompt: A theatre has 12 rows with 15 seats in each row. How many seats are there in total? Show your strategy.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Buses to the city centre leave every 15 minutes and buses to the mall leave every 20 minutes. If both leave together at 8:00 AM, when will they next leave together?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A farmer has 48 apples and 60 oranges. He makes baskets that each hold the same number of apples and the same number of oranges, using all the fruit. What is the largest number of baskets he can make, and how much of each fruit goes in a basket?
realProblems.answer: LCM of 15 and 20 is 60, so 60 minutes later, at 9:00 AM.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: LCM of 15 and 20 is 60, so 60 minutes later, at 9:00 AM.

realProblems.prompt: A book has 28 pages and each page has 15 lines. How many lines are there in the whole book?
realProblems.answer: HCF of 48 and 60 is 12, so 12 baskets, each with 4 apples and 5 oranges.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: HCF of 48 and 60 is 12, so 12 baskets, each with 4 apples and 5 oranges.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find the HCF of 24 and 36. Show the factor lists you used.
Find the LCM of 6 and 8. Show the multiple lists you used.
A chocolate bar has 24 squares. Draw or describe every rectangular arrangement of the squares, and say which factor pair each one shows.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1×24, 2×12, 3×8, 4×6 – each is a factor pair of 24.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A teacher has 30 pupils and wants equal groups with none left over. What are all the possible group sizes? Explain how factors help you answer.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Factors of 30: 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 15, 30, so the possible group sizes are 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 15 and 30; every group size must divide 30 exactly, which is why we use its factors.

Let us picture the factors of 12 with arrays of stars. Each different rectangle shows a different factor pair:

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in The Link Between Factors, Multiples and Division.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Factors and multiples are two sides of the same coin, and they are tied tightly to division. Hold on to these two golden sentences: If a × b = c, then a and b are factors of c, and c is a multiple of both a and b.

questions.question: Work out 8 × 7.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
questions.explanation: If you do not recall it instantly, use known facts. 5 × 7 = 35 and 3 × 7 = 21, and 5 + 3 = 8, so 8 × 7 = 35 + 21 = 56. You can check with commutativity: 7 × 8 is the same, and 7 × 8 = 56. Both agree.

questions.question: Work out 7 × 28.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
questions.explanation: Partition 28 into 20 + 8. Then 7 × 20 = 140 and 7 × 8 = 56. Add them: 140 + 56 = 196. This is the distributive property doing the hard work for you.

questions.question: Use factors to work out 63 ÷ 7.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
questions.explanation: Ask, what times 7 makes 63? From the sevens table, 9 × 7 = 63. So 7 is a factor of 63 and 63 ÷ 7 = 9. Division and multiplication are inverse operations.

questions.question: What does “Factor” mean?
options: A number that divides another exactly
The result of a times-table step
Two factors that multiply to the number
questions.answer: A number that divides another exactly

rounds.explanation: If you do not recall it instantly, use known facts. 5 × 7 = 35 and 3 × 7 = 21, and 5 + 3 = 8, so 8 × 7 = 35 + 21 = 56. You can check with commutativity: 7 × 8 is the same, and 7 × 8 = 56. Both agree.

rounds.explanation: Multiple means The result of a times-table step.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
rounds.explanation: Partition 28 into 20 + 8. Then 7 × 20 = 140 and 7 × 8 = 56. Add them: 140 + 56 = 196. This is the distributive property doing the hard work for you.

rounds.explanation: Product means The answer to a multiplication.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 5 methods.
rounds.explanation: Ask, what times 7 makes 63? From the sevens table, 9 × 7 = 63. So 7 is a factor of 63 and 63 ÷ 7 = 9. Division and multiplication are inverse operations.

selfAssessment: I can recall and use multiplication facts up to 12 × 12, with special confidence in the 7 times table.
I can identify and generate multiples of any given number.
I can find and list all the factors of a given number in a systematic way.
I can find common multiples of two or more numbers, and name the lowest common multiple (LCM).
I can find common factors of two or more numbers, and name the highest common factor (HCF).
I can use arrays to picture multiplication, factors and factor pairs.
I can apply efficient mental multiplication strategies such as partitioning, doubling and halving, and the distributive property.
I can solve multi-step and real-world problems involving multiplication, factors and multiples.
